sentences.base_language: I started learning Korean six months ago.
sentences.romanized: Jeoneun yukgaewol jeone hangugeo reul baeugi sijakhaesseoyo.
sentences.literal: I[topic] six-months ago[at] Korean[obj] learn-beginning started.
sentences.grammarNote: Verb stem + -기 시작하다 means 'to start doing': 배우기 시작했어요 = 'started learning'. The -기 ending nominalises the verb (배우기 = the act of learning), and 시작하다 means to begin.

sentences.base_language: First I learned Hangul, the Korean alphabet.
sentences.grammarNote:
**인** (in) = 'which is / that is' — a short relative clause particle: 알파벳인 한글 = 'Hangul, which is the alphabet.' It's the modifier form of 이다 (to be).
**한글** (Hangeul) was created in 1443 by King Sejong. It consists of 14 consonants and 10 vowels combined into syllable blocks.
**배우다** (baeuda) = 'to learn' — past tense: 배웠어요. Compare: 가르치다 (to teach), 공부하다 (to study), 배우다 (to learn from someone/something).

sentences.base_language: Hangul is actually easy to learn.
sentences.grammarNote: Verb stem + -기 쉽다/어렵다 means 'easy/difficult to do': 배우기 쉬워요 (easy to learn), 쓰기 어려워요 (difficult to write). The -기 nominalises the verb so it can be described by an adjective.

sentences.base_language: But grammar is quite challenging for me.
sentences.grammarNote:
**저한테** (jeonhante) = 'for me / to me' — 저 (polite 'I') + 한테 (particle meaning 'to/for a person'). The more formal equivalent is **저에게** (jeоege).
**하지만** (hajiman) = 'however / but' — a sentence-initial connector. In mid-sentence, **-지만** attaches to the verb/adjective stem.
**어렵다** (eoryeopda) = 'to be difficult' — an irregular ㅂ verb: 어려워요 (the ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel ending).

sentences.base_language: I watch Korean dramas with Korean subtitles.
sentences.grammarNote:
**으로/로** marks the means or method: 자막으로 = 'using subtitles / with subtitles.' After consonants: -으로; after vowels or ㄹ: -로.
**자막** (jamak) = subtitles — 자 (字, characters) + 막 (幕, screen/curtain).
**보다** (boda) = 'to see / watch' — polite present: 봐요 (봐 is the contracted form of 보아). Used for watching TV, movies, and looking at things.
Watching content with Korean subtitles (한국어 자막) is a popular language learning method endorsed by many Korean learners.

sentences.base_language: It helps me understand natural Korean speech.
sentences.target_language: 자연스러운 한국어를 이해하는 데 도움이 돼요.
sentences.romanized: Jayeonseureo-un hangugeoreul ihaehaneun de doumi dwaeyo.
sentences.grammarNote: Verb stem + -는 데 도움이 되다 means 'it helps in doing': 이해하는 데 도움이 돼요 = 'it helps in understanding'. The -는 데 clause converts a verb into a noun phrase meaning 'the act of' or 'in doing'.

sentences.base_language: Every week I go to a language exchange meetup.
sentences.grammarNote:
**매주** (maeju) = 'every week' — 매 (每, every) + 주 (週, week). Similar forms: 매일 (every day), 매달 (every month), 매년 (every year).
**언어 교환** (eoneo gyohwan) = 'language exchange' — 언어 (language) + 교환 (交換, exchange).
**모임** (moim) = meeting/gathering — from 모이다 (to gather). The destination particle **에** marks where one goes: 모임에 가다 (go to the meetup).

sentences.base_language: I practice speaking Korean with native speakers.
sentences.grammarNote:
**원어민** (woneоmin) = native speaker — 원 (原, original/native) + 어 (語, language) + 민 (民, people).
**말하기** (malhagi) = 'speaking' as a noun — verb 말하다 (to speak) + -기 nominaliser. The four skills: 말하기 (speaking), 듣기 (listening), 읽기 (reading), 쓰기 (writing).
**과** (gwa) = 'with / and' after a consonant: 원어민과 (with a native speaker). After vowels: **와** (wa).
**연습을 하다** (yeonseup-eul hada) = 'to practice' — 연습 (練習, practice) + 을 하다.

해 주다 (hae juda) = 'to do (something) for (someone)'. The -아/어 주다 structure emphasises that an action is a favour or service done for the benefit of another person. It is one of Korean's core social grammar constructions.

sentences.base_language: My Korean is improving every day.
sentences.target_language: 제 한국어 실력이 매일 늘고 있어요.
sentences.romanized: Je hangugeo sillyeogi maeil neulgo isseoyo.
sentences.literal: My Korean skill[subj] every-day growing is.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 있어요** (-go isseoyo) = present progressive: 늘고 있어요 = 'is growing / is improving' (ongoing action).
**실력** (sillyeok) = 'skill / proficiency / ability' — commonly used for language ability: 한국어 실력 (Korean proficiency).
**늘다** (neulda) = 'to increase / grow / improve' — an intransitive verb used for skills, amounts, and numbers: 실력이 늘다 (skills improve), 사람이 늘다 (people increase).
Its transitive counterpart is **늘리다** (neullida) = 'to increase something': 실력을 늘리다 (improve one's skills).

sentences.base_language: I will not stop until I am fluent.
sentences.romanized: Yuchang haejil ttaekkaji meomchuji aneul geoyeyo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**때까지** (ttaekkaji) = 'until (the time when)' — V-(으)ㄹ 때까지 = 'until (something) happens': 유창해질 때까지 = 'until I become fluent.'
**유창하다** (yuchang-hada) = 'to be fluent' — 유창해지다 = 'to become fluent' (-지다 makes a change-of-state verb).
**-지 않을 거예요** = 'will not do' — negative future: 멈추지 않을 거예요 = 'will not stop.'
**까지** (kkaji) = 'until/up to' marks an endpoint, contrasting with **부터** (from/since) which marks a starting point.
